item.title: The cartographers who mapped clouds
item.content:encoded: In the 1800s a small society tried to chart the sky as if it were land. They drew “coastlines” of cumulus and named the bays of stratus.

item.title: Why medieval bells had names
item.content:encoded: Every great bell was baptized. A bell named “Gabriel” rang for births; another, for storms.

item.title: The lost color of Tyrian purple
item.content:encoded: It took ten thousand sea snails to dye a single robe the color of dried blood and shadow.

item.title: Bees that count to four
item.content:encoded: Honeybees can solve simple arithmetic, trading sugar for the right answer.

item.title: The library of smells
item.content:encoded: An archive in the Netherlands preserves the smells of disappearing trades.

item.title: Clocks that ran on candles
item.content:encoded: Before springs and pendulums, time was something you burned.

item.title: The whale that spoke like a person
item.content:encoded: NOC, a beluga, lowered his pitch to mimic human speech and startled his trainers.

item.title: Maps with monsters in the margins
item.content:encoded: The phrase “here be dragons” is rarer than you think; mapmakers preferred sea-pigs.

item.title: The town that banned darkness
item.content:encoded: A Norwegian valley installed giant mirrors to throw sunlight into its winter square.

item.title: Fish that farm algae
item.content:encoded: Damselfish weed their gardens, evict intruders, and tend a single favored crop.

item.title: The forgotten art of memory palaces
item.content:encoded: Ancient orators walked imaginary buildings to recall hours of speech.

item.title: The secret math of snowflakes (paid)
item.content:encoded:
Here is a taste of why no two are alike...

This post is for paid subscribers.
